questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the meaning of the term 'exercise adherence' as commonly used in sport and health psychology literature?

options.text: The degree to which an individual consistently follows a planned exercise programme over time
The one-time completion of a single fitness test at the beginning
The ability to avoid all forms of physical activity completely
The skill of performing a single perfect technical movement

questions.explanation: Exercise adherence describes the degree to which an individual consistently follows a planned exercise programme over time; high adherence means the person sticks to the frequency, intensity, duration and type of exercise prescribed by the plan for health gains.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: One-time test completion is not adherence to a programme.
Avoiding all physical activity is the opposite of adherence.
A single perfect movement is a skill, not adherence.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most accurate general definition of aggression as commonly used in the context of sports psychology and athlete behaviour?

options.text: Behaviour intended to cause harm to another living being physically or psychologically
A routine friendly handshake performed before any sports match
A purely random unintended event that occurs during a play
The display of excellent sportsmanship between rival teams

questions.explanation: Aggression in sport psychology is defined as behaviour intended to cause harm to another living being physically or psychologically; the key element is intent to cause harm, which distinguishes aggression from accidental injuries or legitimate competitive contact in sport.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: A handshake is a friendly ritual, not aggression.
Random unintended events lack the required intent to harm.
Good sportsmanship is the opposite of aggressive behaviour.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is one of the most common physical health benefits typically associated with long-term adherence to a regular planned exercise programme in adults?

options.text: Reduced risk of cardiovascular disease
Permanent loss of all body water content
Complete elimination of sleep for days
Total replacement of any healthy diet plan

questions.explanation: Long-term adherence to regular exercise is strongly linked to reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, better blood pressure control, healthier body composition, stronger bones and improved mental wellbeing; these gains require sustained, not occasional, activity over time.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Loss of all body water is dangerous dehydration, not benefit.
Healthy exercise supports rather than eliminates sleep.
Exercise complements, not replaces, a healthy diet plan.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is one of the most common psychological benefits associated with regular exercise adherence in adults as described in mental health and sport psychology literature?

options.text: Reduced symptoms of mild-to-moderate anxiety and depression
Complete elimination of all emotions permanently
Inability to form any social relationships
Total loss of interest in any new activities

questions.explanation: Regular exercise is associated with reduced symptoms of mild-to-moderate anxiety and depression, improved mood, better self-esteem and enhanced cognitive functioning; these benefits arise through neurobiological and psychosocial mechanisms that accumulate with consistent participation.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Emotions are not eliminated by exercise in healthy people.
Exercise often supports, not hinders, social relationships.
Regular exercise typically expands, not shrinks, interests.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate strategy recommended to help a new exerciser improve long-term adherence to a fitness programme across a year?

options.text: Start with extremely intense workouts that last multiple hours
Set realistic goals and introduce variety, social support and tracking
Keep the routine entirely unchanged for many years
Punish the exerciser for every missed session

questions.explanation: Recommended strategies for adherence include setting realistic goals, introducing variety to prevent boredom, building in social support through exercise partners or groups, and tracking progress with logs or apps; these approaches support sustained motivation and behavioural change.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Very intense long workouts lead to burnout in beginners.
Unchanged routines may cause plateaus and boredom.
Punishment tends to reduce motivation and adherence.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes hostile aggression in the context of competitive sports as commonly defined in sport psychology textbooks?

options.text: Aggression whose main aim is to cause harm or injury to the opponent
Aggression used only as a means to score legitimate points in play
Friendly teasing between teammates with no harmful intent
Accidental collisions in a fast-moving game situation

questions.explanation: Hostile aggression is aggression whose main aim is to cause harm or injury to the opponent; it is driven by anger and has harm itself as its primary goal; it is generally condemned in sport psychology and by sport governing bodies for being outside fair play.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Aggression aimed at scoring points describes instrumental aggression.
Friendly teasing between teammates is not aggression.
Accidental collisions lack the required harmful intent.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes instrumental aggression in competitive sport as commonly defined in sport psychology literature for practising coaches?

options.text: Aggressive behaviour with the primary aim of harming the opponent
Aggressive behaviour used as a means to achieve a legitimate sporting goal
Completely random physical contact with no underlying reason
A celebration of a goal by the whole victorious team

questions.explanation: Instrumental aggression refers to aggressive behaviour used as a means to achieve a legitimate sporting goal, such as a hard but legal tackle in rugby to regain possession of the ball; harm is not the primary aim, and such actions usually stay within the rules.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Primary aim to harm opponents defines hostile aggression.
Random contact lacks the required purposeful intent.
Team celebrations are not aggressive behaviour.

questions.question_text: Which of the following common causes is most likely to raise the level of aggression in athletes during a closely contested competitive match in a team sport?

options.text: Frustration from blocked goals and perceived unfair officiating
Consistently applying deep breathing exercises every few minutes
Listening to calming music while performing the sport
Strictly adhering to the official rules throughout the match

questions.explanation: Frustration from blocked goals and perceived unfair officiating commonly raises aggression in athletes during competitive matches; the frustration-aggression hypothesis suggests that thwarted goal pursuit increases the likelihood of aggressive responses during play in such closely contested situations.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Deep breathing exercises lower, not raise, aggression.
Calming music is used to reduce arousal and aggression.
Strict rule adherence is associated with controlled behaviour.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is one of the most effective general strategies suggested in sport psychology literature for the management and control of aggression in athletes?

options.text: Encouraging athletes to insult opponents more frequently during play
Teaching relaxation techniques, anger management and fair-play values
Ignoring aggressive outbursts and hoping they will go away on their own
Allowing athletes to bring weapons onto the playing field for self-defence

questions.explanation: Recommended strategies to manage aggression include teaching relaxation techniques, structured anger management, positive self-talk, modelling of fair-play values by coaches, and applying clear disciplinary consequences; these address causes and responses at both individual and team levels.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Insulting opponents increases rather than manages aggression.
Ignoring outbursts allows them to escalate over time.
Weapons on the field are obviously dangerous and unlawful.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why a coach might use imagery and relaxation techniques to help an athlete manage aggression during competitive sports events?

options.text: These techniques increase physical aggression toward opponents
These techniques help regulate arousal and emotional responses to triggers
These techniques replace all the need for physical fitness training
These techniques completely eliminate all emotions permanently

questions.explanation: Imagery and relaxation techniques help regulate arousal and emotional responses to provocative triggers, allowing the athlete to maintain focus, remain within the rules and prevent hostile aggression; they work alongside, not instead of, physical training and are ethically grounded.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: These techniques reduce rather than increase physical aggression.
Mental skills complement, not replace, physical training.
Healthy emotion regulation does not eliminate all emotions.

questions.question_text: Match each benefit of exercise adherence in Column A with the correct category description listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.
questions.explanation: Reduced cardiovascular disease risk is a physical health benefit; better mood and lower anxiety are psychological benefits; stronger friendships and social support are a social benefit of group exercise; and improved sleep quality reflects a rest and recovery benefit.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Cardiovascular disease reduction is clearly a physical benefit.
Mood and anxiety reduction are psychological benefits.
Sleep improvement relates to rest and recovery benefits.

questions.question_text: Match each scenario in Column A with the most appropriate type of aggression listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination as per sport psychology classification.

column_a.text: A rugby player tackles hard within the rules to regain the ball
A footballer intentionally kicks an opponent after a whistle
A boxer lands a legitimate legal punch in a bout
A player punches another player in anger after a foul

questions.explanation: A hard but legal tackle to regain the ball is instrumental aggression; intentional kicking after the whistle is hostile aggression; a legitimate legal punch in boxing is instrumental aggression within the rules; an angry punch after a foul is hostile aggression aimed at causing harm.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Hard but legal contact within rules is usually instrumental aggression.
Deliberate harm after the whistle reflects hostile aggression.
Angry post-foul punches exemplify hostile aggression.

questions.question_text: Match each barrier to exercise adherence in Column A with the most appropriate practical strategy to overcome it listed in Column B and choose the correct combination.

column_a.text: Lack of time in a busy working day
Boredom with the same fixed routine
Not seeing clear progress or gains

column_b.text: Join a group or find an exercise partner
Schedule short workouts that fit the daily routine
Introduce variety in activities and cross-training
Track progress with a log or smartphone app

questions.explanation: Lack of time is addressed by scheduling short workouts that fit the daily routine; boredom by introducing variety and cross-training; exercising alone by joining a group or finding a partner; and lack of visible progress by tracking with a log or smartphone app.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Scheduling short workouts directly addresses time barriers.
Variety and cross-training are classic boredom remedies.
Tracking progress is the go-to fix for invisible progress.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about exercise adherence and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in health psychology practice.
questions.assertion: High exercise adherence is generally needed to gain long-term physical and psychological benefits from a training programme.
questions.reason: Most chronic adaptations such as cardiovascular improvement and mood regulation require consistent exercise over months and years.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Most chronic cardiovascular, metabolic and psychological adaptations require consistent exercise sustained over months and years; this biological need for sustained stimulus directly explains why high exercise adherence is required to achieve long-term health and fitness gains.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly explains chronic adaptation needs.
Both statements align with exercise physiology evidence.
Consistent stimulus genuinely drives long-term adaptations.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about aggression in competitive sport and choose the option that best represents their logical connection in sport psychology.
questions.assertion: Hostile aggression in competitive sport is generally condemned and often sanctioned by sport governing bodies around the world.
questions.reason: Hostile aggression has harm as its primary aim, which is incompatible with the values of fair play, safety and sportsmanship.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Hostile aggression aims primarily at harm, which is incompatible with the values of fair play, safety and sportsmanship; this incompatibility directly explains why sport governing bodies condemn such behaviour and apply sanctions to those who engage in it during competition.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly explains the fair play rationale.
Both statements align with sport governance practice.
Harm-seeking behaviour is clearly incompatible with fair play.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about aggression management and choose the option that best describes their logical connection in sport psychology intervention.
questions.assertion: Anger management training can reduce the frequency of hostile aggression in youth sport over the course of a competitive season.
questions.reason: Anger management training teaches cognitive reappraisal, relaxation and alternative behaviour choices when provocations occur during play.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Cognitive reappraisal, relaxation skills and alternative behaviour choices give athletes new ways to respond to provocations during play; applied consistently, these tools can reduce hostile aggression over a season, directly explaining the effect of anger management training described in the assertion.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason captures the core training mechanisms.
Both statements align with intervention research.
New response options directly reduce aggressive outbursts.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages in the correct chronological order when a coach designs an exercise adherence strategy for a newly recruited group of adult beginners.

items.text: Assess participants' initial fitness and goals
Design a realistic, varied programme with social support
Monitor adherence and provide regular feedback
Adjust programme based on feedback and progress

options.text: P → Q → R → S
Q → P → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: The coach first assesses participants' initial fitness and goals; then designs a realistic, varied programme with social support; then monitors adherence and provides regular feedback during the season; and finally adjusts the programme based on feedback and progress.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Initial assessment must precede any programme design.
Monitoring and feedback follow initial implementation.
Adjustment is based on later feedback, not first action.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages in the correct chronological order when a coach designs an intervention to manage and control aggression in a youth team over a full season.

items.text: Identify common aggression triggers in the team
Teach relaxation, anger management and fair-play values
Monitor behaviour and give specific behavioural feedback
Apply clear disciplinary consequences and rewards consistently

options.text: P → Q → S → R
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: The coach first identifies common aggression triggers in the team; teaches relaxation, anger management and fair-play values; applies clear disciplinary consequences and rewards consistently; and then monitors behaviour and gives specific behavioural feedback over the season.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Identifying triggers precedes teaching management skills.
Skill teaching precedes consistent discipline and reward.
Monitoring and feedback run after rules are applied.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: A hard but fully legal tackle whose primary aim is to regain possession of the ball is usually classified as instrumental aggression in sport psychology.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: A hard but fully legal tackle whose primary aim is to regain possession of the ball is usually classified as instrumental aggression in sport psychology; harm is not the primary aim, and the behaviour remains within the rules while still applying firm contact in play.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Instrumental aggression uses contact as a means, not as harm goal.
Legal tackles fit the instrumental aggression definition.
Regaining possession is a legitimate sporting goal.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram summarising types of aggression in sport with their descriptions and examples, and identify the type of aggression that has harm to the opponent as its primary aim.

options.text: Hostile aggression
Instrumental aggression
Assertive behaviour within rules
Accidental unintentional contact

questions.explanation: Hostile aggression has harm to the opponent as its primary aim; instrumental aggression uses firm contact only as a means to a legitimate sporting goal; assertive behaviour stays within the rules and values, and accidental contact lacks the intent required for any form of aggression.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Instrumental aggression does not treat harm as the primary aim.
Assertive behaviour stays within fair play values.
Accidental contact does not involve any intent to harm.
